coredata.dc:description: Current fluorescent labels are often expensive and environmentally unfriendly and tend to lose their fluorescence when combined with dry and hydrophilic substrates because of their hydrophobic nature. Thus, the development of environmentally friendly, low-cost, and scalable fluorescent materials is still highly desirable but significantly challenging, especially for anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring. In this study, highly fluorescent nanoparticles were prepared by mimicking organelles and using natural and sustainable curcumin as a fluorescent component. The preparation process was easily scalable via traditional seeded emulsion polymerization methods and green without using additional organic solvents. The resulting curcumin-based fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) were well dispersed in water, offering smart fluorescence in water-based environments or a dried polymer coating. In particular, CFNs-based coatings could be used for effective smart anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring. The proposed strategies may inspire the design of novel, green, and sustainable smart fluorescent materials/coatings for applications in anti-counterfeiting, smart food tags and labels, and visual sensing systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A Schematic showing curcumin-based fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) preparation for anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring; b scanning electron microscopy images of pristine polymer nanoparticles and CFNs; and c transmission electron microscopy images of the CFNs.

A Photographs of the CFNs solution with slight separation after remaining still for one month. b–e Zeta-potentials of the b supernatants of the CFNs solution after centrifugation at various speeds, c supernatants of the CFNs solution (centrifuged at 2500 rpm) after several numbers of dilutions, d nanoparticles with the addition of various amounts of AA, and e nanoparticles with the addition of various amounts of APS. f Schematic showing the double-layer model of the CFNs. g Photographs of the CFNs at various pH values before (top row) and after (bottom row) remaining still for one week.

A Fluorescent photographs and b the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the CFNs supernatants after centrifugation at various speeds. c Fluorescent photographs of the CFNs solution (original concentration: 40 mg/mL) after various numbers of dilutions. d Optical photographs of the freeze-dried CFNs on a plastic lattice without (left) and with (right) UV light exposure. e Photographs of the CFNs with various pH values under natural light (top panel) and UV light with long (middle panel) and short (bottom panel) photographic exposure times. Fluorescence was observed using a UV light of 365 nm in all the photographs.

Various demonstrations of smart fluorescence properties of the as-fabricated CFNs. a Photographs and b the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent hydrogels obtained using various concentrations of CFNs (0, 10, 25, and 50 μL of CFNs in 1 mL hydrogel precursor). c Photographs demonstrating the fluorescence loss when in contact with ammonia solution and its reappearance after ammonia evaporation, and d photographs showing color changes after high-temperature treatment at 100 °C for 4 h as well as e their corresponding fluorescence spectra. f The fluorescence intensities at λem = 490 nm of fluorescence PU in contact with ammonia solution and after ammonia evaporation for various numbers of cycles. g Plot of transparency (%) against wavelength for the polymer film with 20 μL CFNs, along with inset photographs demonstrating the fluorescent behavior. h Photographs showing the coating prepared with 20 μL CFNs, an irregular and opaque light-yellow film under natural lighting, while the underlying pattern was clearly revealed by green fluorescence under UV light. i Photographs of using the precursor solution to write on the surface of the as-prepared coating with a brush and the strong green fluorescence of the cured coating under UV light. j Photographs of florescent writing for information anti-counterfeiting, where the writing exhibited a strong green fluorescence under UV light. It then changed to blue fluorescence after treatment with water (20 μL CFNs in 1 mL PU precursor).

Application to food quality monitoring. a Photographs showing the fluorescence of the CFNs-loaded papers over the course of 12 h in the absence (top row) and presence of bacteria at 104, 106, and 108 CFUs. b Bar chart showing the fluorescent illuminance of the CFNs coating after 12 h in the presence of 0, 104, 106, and 108 CFUs of E. coli bacteria. c Photographs of pork stored in a fluorescent film, where the fluorescence changed from green to blue in response to the deterioration of the pork. d Comparison of this work with some literature [62,63,64,65,35].

(a) Photographs of the CFNs with some typical components of food, such as salts, sugar, and Vitamin C, with a concentration of 2 mg/mL and (b) the corresponding fluorescent spectra. (c) Photographs of the CFNs with various amounts of FeCl3 and (d) the corresponding fluorescent spectra. (e) Photographs of the CFNs with various amounts of CuCl2 and (f) the corresponding fluorescent spectra. (g) The metal complex structures of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with curcumin.

head.ce:title: Organelle-like water-borne curcumin nanoparticles with smart fluorescence for anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current fluorescent labels are often expensive and environmentally unfriendly and tend to lose their fluorescence when combined with dry and hydrophilic substrates because of their hydrophobic nature. Thus, the development of environmentally friendly, low-cost, and scalable fluorescent materials is still highly desirable but significantly challenging, especially for anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring. In this study, highly fluorescent nanoparticles were prepared by mimicking organelles and using natural and sustainable curcumin as a fluorescent component. The preparation process was easily scalable via traditional seeded emulsion polymerization methods and green without using additional organic solvents. The resulting curcumin-based fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs) were well dispersed in water, offering smart fluorescence in water-based environments or a dried polymer coating. In particular, CFNs-based coatings could be used for effective smart anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring. The proposed strategies may inspire the design of novel, green, and sustainable smart fluorescent materials/coatings for applications in anti-counterfeiting, smart food tags and labels, and visual sensing systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials that respond to external compounds and stimuli are important in a broad range of industries and everyday applications, including agriculture [1], anti-counterfeiting [2,3], and food quality monitoring [4,5]. For instance, fluorescent materials can exhibit florescent colors or intensity changes under external stimuli such as temperature, irradiation, or pH [6,7,8,9]. Therefore, stored information can be observed via a vibrant display to protect important and valuable data and items from replication, thereby eliminating risks to global trade, human health, and national security [9,10,11,12,13]. Additionally, food quality can be monitored using real-time fluorescence changes, thus addressing food safety problems and protecting human health [14]. Various fluorescent materials have been developed, including nanocrystals [11], organic dyes [15], lanthanide-ion-doped luminescent materials [16], aggregation-induced emission fluorescent agents [17], and carbon dots (CDs) [18]. However, these fluorescent materials are often toxic and unsustainable, and their preparation involves low yield, high cost, and high time/energy consumption [19,20,21]. Moreover, the materials are often monofunctional, exhibiting only one fluorescent color under ultraviolet (UV) light [22,23]; therefore, they can be easily replicated by mimicking their specific colors. Numerous strategies have been proposed to achieve high-level security via fluorescent color changes. These include (i) combining multiple fluorescent agents (for example, three distinct dyes [24]) or incorporating multiple lanthanide ions (such as Eu3+ and Tb3+) or incorporating CDs [25] to obtain a material with multiple fluorescent colors; (ii) constructing nano/micro-structured patterns with multiple colors that are induced via diffraction, scattering, or refection, although this is difficult and complicated to achieve on soft materials [26]; and (iii) tuning interactions between the fluorescent agents and the environment. As an example of the last strategy, CDs have been functionalized with vinyl groups and then introduced into a glycol gel to provide blue fluorescence that changes to rose-red via solvent exchange with water [27]; however, the method involved low yields and high cost, and the printed pattern might disappear. Hence, a simple, low-cost, green, and scalable procedure for the preparation of smart fluorescent materials is highly desirable to address the global demand for long-lasting, high-security anti-counterfeiting and food safety monitoring materials.

Curcumin is a widely available and naturally occurring compound. It has been used for several thousand years [28,29,30] owing to its excellent biocompatibility and anti-diabetic, anti-inflammatory, and anti-cancerous functions. Its large conjugated structure can emit green fluorescence in the wavelength range of 450–650 nm in organic solvents, thus offering potential usage as a fluorescent label in anti-counterfeiting applications [31,32,33,34,35]. However, because of the unstable diketone structure, the fluorescence of curcumin is highly sensitive to solvent polarity; that is, it is readily observed in organic solvents but difficult to maintain in water/dried polymer coatings [33,34,35,36]. Therefore, developing a strategy to maintain the strong fluorescence of curcumin in both aqueous solutions and dried polymer coatings is essential for expanding its applications as low-cost, sustainable, green, and smart fluorescent materials for anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring.

Living cells and their organelles are complex entities with independent internal microenvironments to protect themselves from exterior stimuli (e.g., high salt concentration) via cell walls. Their microenvironments can be modulated based on specific requirements [37], for example, generating an acidic microenvironment for lysosomes to hydrolyze molecules/biomacromolecules [38]. Inspired by such living cells, organelle-like nanoparticles with hydrophilic and hydrophobic segments were constructed for this study via a simple and facile seeded emulsion polymerization process. To protect the strong fluorescence of curcumin from the water environment, curcumin was loaded into nanoparticles to obtain water-borne smart curcumin-based fluorescent nanoparticles (CFNs). The preparation process was facile and scalable, and the obtained CFNs exhibited good dispersion in water. The fluorescent color changed from green to blue depending on the polarity of the environment. Moreover, the CFNs possessed strong fluorescence in both aqueous solutions and dried polymer coatings. Additionally, the potential of the CFNs for anti-counterfeiting was demonstrated as the strong green fluorescence of the coating changed to blue under UV light and water treatment. The fluorescence also responded to the ammonia (NH3) produced by bacteria, enabling food quality monitoring. The proposed strategy provides a new and effective route for the low-cost, green, scalable, and simple fabrication of smart fluorescent materials for applications in fluorescent polymer coatings, visual sensing systems, information storage, anti-counterfeiting, and smart food tags and labels.

All chemicals used were of reagent grade and used without further purification. Methyl methacrylate (MMA), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL), copper chloride (CuCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), and L-Ascorbic acid (vitamin C) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Sugar was purchased from Guangzhou Chuangyi Food Co., Ltd. (China). Acrylic acid (AA) and butyl acrylate (BA) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ammonium persulfate (APS), ferric chloride (FeCl3), and potassium sulfate (K2SO4) were supplied by Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Curcumin was obtained from Shanghai Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (China). Poly(tetramethylene ether)glycol (PTMG) was obtained from Jining Huakai Resin Co., Ltd. (China). Isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) was provided by Hengtai Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Dimethylacetamide (DMAc) was obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Kermel Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli; ATCC 25922) was supplied by Shanghai Luwei Microbial Sci. & Tech. Co., Ltd. (China); Luria-Bertani (LB) agar and nutrient broth (NB) were purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbiology Technology Co., Ltd. (China); and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was obtained from Xiamen Haibao Technology Co., Ltd. (China).

The diameters of the nanoparticles were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer 2000 instrument (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an fluorescent spectrophotometer (F-4500, HITACHI, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer. The morphologies of the nanoparticles were observed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM; JEOL JSM-7001F) and field-emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JEM-2100F) at 200 kV. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra was measured by an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (U-2910, HITACHI, Japan).

The polymer nanoparticle emulsion was prepared by seeded emulsion polymerization. Typically, 3.0 wt% APS solution (2 mL) was added dropwise to a mixture of MMA (2 mL), BA (2 mL), and distilled water (63 mL) at 80 °C under vigorous stirring for up to 1 h to obtain a seeded emulsion. Thereafter, a 4.0 wt% APS solution (2 mL) and a mixture of the hydrophilic monomer AA (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, or 0.4 g) and hydrophobic monomers MMA (2 mL) and BA (2 mL) were added dropwise to the seeded emulsion and allowed to polymerize for 2 h to obtain a polymer nanoparticle emulsion. All aforementioned reactions were performed under condensation and reflux with stirring at 80 °C.

To synthesize a water-borne CFNs solution, curcumin (0.10 g) was added to the polymer nanoparticle emulsion and allowed to react for 2 h. The resulting yellow solution was filtered through gauze and centrifuged at 1000 rpm to remove excess curcumin. The concentration of the CFNs solution was determined to be 40 mg/mL by drying 1 mL of the solution on a tin foil.

To vary the size of the nanoparticles, the as-prepared solutions were further centrifuged at 2500, 5000, 7500, and 10000 rpm. All aforementioned reactions were performed under condensation and reflux with stirring at 80 °C.

Polyurethane (PU) precursors were prepared by first adding PEG (1.0 mmol), PTMG (4.0 mmol), IPDI (15.0 mmol), DMAc (20.0 mL), and the catalyst DBTDL (20.0 μL) into a three-necked flask. The mixture was heated to 80 °C while stirring with a constant speed of 200 rpm, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 5 h. After cooling to 40 °C, a mixture of water (10.0 mmol) and DMAc (40.0 mL) was added. The reaction was then continued at 40 °C for 21 h to yield the PU precursor, which formed the PU coating after curing at 80 °C.

The as-prepared CFNs solution (50 μL) was added to the PU precursor (5.00 g) under vigorous stirring for 5 min to obtain a fluorescent CFNs/PU solution. The solution was then coated on a glass substrate and dried in an oven at 100 °C for 5 min to obtain a fluorescent polymer coating.

Acrylamide (AAm) (2.5 g), APS (0.005 g), and N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA) (0.005 g) were dissolved in deionized water (7.5 g) and sonicated to obtain a homogeneous precursor solution. The mixtures containing the precursor solution (1 mL) and CFNs (0, 10, 25, or 50 μL) were transferred into a mold and heated at 60 °C for 4 h to form hydrogels.

To prepare gram-negative E. coli cultures, LB agar, and NBs were prepared according to the protocol in the Product Operation Manual (LB agar medium: 36 g/L, NB medium: 18 g/L). Single colonies of E. coli were then incubated in the NB at 37 °C for 24 h while shaking at 180 rpm. The bacteria were then diluted to a series of concentrations of 105, 107, and 109 colony forming units (CFU)/mL with PBS (0.02 M). Subsequently, 100 µL of diluted bacterial suspension was evenly transferred to a solid LB agar plate. In addition, a separate Petri dish containing NB without bacteria was prepared as a control; this blank solution was also added to a solid LB agar plate. Finally, test strips of filter paper containing the as-fabricated CFNs (2 mg) were placed in the solid LB agar plates containing 0, 104, 106, and 108 CFUs of bacteria and incubated at 37 °C for 0–12 h. The changes in fluorescence of the test strip were observed after 6, 9, and 12 h. The illuminance of the test strips under UV light was measured using a luminance meter (AS-803, SMART SENSOR).

To fabricate CFNs, a polymer nanoparticle emulsion was first prepared using a facile and simple seeded emulsion polymerization technology. As shown in Fig. 1 a, hydrophobic monomers MMA and BA were dispersed in an aqueous solution with vigorous stirring to form droplets; then, polymerization was initiated in the presence of APS to generate hydrophobic nanoparticle seeds. Thereafter, APS, MMA, BA, and AA were added dropwise to the polymer solution to grow larger nanoparticles, and a white liquid product was obtained after 2 h (Fig. S1a).

Next, curcumin was added to the emulsion and continuously stirred for 2 h to obtain a water-borne yellow CFNs solution (Fig. S1b). It is noteworthy that the CFNs solution was loaded into the polymer nanoparticles via the hydrophobic interaction and the unloaded and physical-dispersed curcumin was removed via centrifugation (1000 rpm). Moreover, curcumin may react with the polymer chains via the esterification reaction between the hydroxy groups of curcumin and the ester groups of pMMA and pBA, or the carboxyl groups of pAA (see Fig. S1c). Thus, the CFN solution was not a simple physical mixture of curcumin and the polymer nanoparticle emulsion, which could not effectively protect the curcumin molecules and provide them hydrophobic environment for strong fluorescence. The successful preparation of CFNs was confirmed by the evident color change. The FTIR spectra (Fig. S2) also showed the characteristic peaks of polymers and curcumin. The morphologies of the pristine polymer nanoparticles and CFNs were further revealed by SEM images, as shown in Fig. 1b. The pristine polymer nanoparticles exhibited smooth surfaces with diameters of ∼250 nm, whereas the CFNs possessed rough surfaces and similar diameters (∼200 nm), in accordance with the DLS results (Fig. S3) and TEM images (Fig. 1c). Moreover, by centrifuging the CFNs solutions at various speeds (1000, 2500, 5000, 7500, and 10000 rpm; Fig. S4), the collected supernatants contained smaller particles, confirmed by TEM images (Fig. S5). Thus, the aforementioned results indicated that curcumin could be effectively loaded into nanoparticles, and the products could be easily collected via centrifugation.

The stability of the fluorescent nanoparticles solution is important for achieving good particle distribution, high pattern resolution, and facile practical fabrication [39,40,41]. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CFNs solution exhibited a slight phase separation after remaining still for one month. However, the precipitates could be redistributed uniformly via simple shaking, indicating its excellent stability. Further, the results shown in Fig. 2b indicated that the zeta potential of the nanoparticles in the supernatant decreased significantly from –1.0 to –27.9 mV as the centrifugal speed was increased from 0 to 10000 rpm. The observed negative potential was attributed to the carboxyl groups of the poly AA (pAA) segments and APS sulfate groups. Furthermore, the carboxyl groups tended to dissociate more at a lower concentration [42,43], which was confirmed by the results shown in Fig. 2c, where the zeta potential of the CFNs solution decreased from –0.577 to –33.767 mV as the dilution factor increased from 0 to 1000.

Additionally, the effects of various amounts of AA and APS addition on the stability of the CFNs were studied by analyzing the zeta potential results (Fig. 2d and 2e). The zeta potential decreased from –40.9 to –46.2 mV as the amount of AA was raised from 0 to 300 mg, although it increased to –34.42 mV when the amount of AA was further raised to 400 mg (Fig. 2d). Thus, the effect of the negatively charged carboxyl groups was confirmed. Contrarily, the zeta potential increased slightly from –38 to –32.1 mV as the amount of APS increased from 70 to 122.5 mg (Fig. 2e); this observation was attributed to the diffused double-layer model shown in Fig. 2f. As pAA was the main component in the particles, increasing the number of carboxyl groups (i.e., the amount of AA) generated more negative charges on the surface, thereby decreasing the zeta potential. Meanwhile, the ammonium cations of APS tended to adsorb to the negatively charged particles to form a diffusion layer, thereby reducing the zeta potential. Finally, the good dispersibility of the CFNs under various pH conditions was shown in Fig. 2g, where no obvious aggregation or separation occurred after the CFNs solution (40 mg/mL, 400 mg AA, ∼122 mg APS) remained still for one week at pH 1–13. Thus, CFNs with 300 mg AA and 70 mg APS were selected for the subsequent experiments.

The fluorescent properties of CFNs are vital for their applications in anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring. The underlying mechanism of the smart fluorescence of CFNs was mainly based on the properties of the curcumin molecules, especially their internal electron-jumping properties and fluorescence emission ability. The occurrence of intramolecular hydrogen atoms transfer at the di-ketone chain of curcumin led to the existence of keto-enol tautomeric conformations in equilibrium (Fig. S6) [44,45,46]. Thus, there are two reversible forms of curcumin molecules: di-keto and keto-enol. Attributed to the large conjugated structures of keto-enol forms, strong long-wavelength fluorescence (λEm: 530 nm) could be emitted and observed, while di-keto would lead to weak fluorescent intensity with short-wavelength fluorescence (λEm: 430 nm) [47]. Therefore, the fluorescent intensities were closely related to the concentration of the relative concentrations of di-keto and keto-enol forms, which could vary according to the polarity of the external environment, such as the polarity of solvent and pH [44,45]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, after centrifugating the CFNs solution at 2500 rpm, the precipitated yellow nanoparticles exhibited strong green fluorescence, even in a water environment. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra obtained at various centrifugation speeds (0–10000 rpm, Fig. 3b) showed a fluorescent peak at approximately 500 nm, indicating an obvious blue shift relative to the characteristic curcumin peak at 537 nm [48,49,50]. The curcumin was loaded in a strongly hydrophobic environment [49,50]. After centrifugation at various speeds, the fluorescent color of the supernatant changed from grass green through cyan to light blue, and the corresponding fluorescent peak gradually shifted to 475 nm. This blue shift was because that the concentration of the CFNs was decreased, indicating the water contents increasing with the higher polarity after centrifugation, and thus the high polarity (high zeta potential) of the environment strengthened the interaction between curcumin and the hydrophobic core and varying the relative concentration of di-keto forms. In addition, with higher centrifugation speed, the dispersion of CFNs increased and agglomeration of CFNs reduced in solution with the water contents increasing after centrifugation, which led to a clearer solution, where light could be transmitted easily with less scattering behaviors. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity increased with the centrifugation speed, and achieved highest at 10,000 rpm centrifugation. To further confirm the influence of polarity of water, the CFNs solution was diluted with various amounts of water to obtain a high polarity index of 10.2 [51]. As shown in Fig. 3c, a similar color change from grass green through cyan to light blue was observed, which also observed in the corresponding fluorescent spectra (Fig. S7), confirming the influence of the high polarity of water on the fluorescence of CFNs by converting to the di-ketone structure. The UV–Vis spectra of the CFNs after various dilution and centrifugation speed also confirmed that the curcumin was effectively protected by the nanoparticles, leading to an absorbance at 419 nm assigned to π–π∗ transitions of the conjugated curcumin (keto-enol form), which then decreased with an increased in the dilution times and centrifugation speed (Fig. S8) [29]. Strong fluorescence in the dried state was demonstrated in Fig. 3d, where the CFNs solution was dropped onto a plastic slide with a lattice pattern, and then water content was removed via vacuum-drying for 24 h. During the vacuum drying process, the droplets were shrined with an increase in the concentrations of residual CFNs droplets, leading to the residual aggregation of CFNs solid at the center of the droplets trace to form bright yellow dots. The obtained yellow areas exhibited strong green fluorescence under UV light, even after drying. Thus, the as-fabricated CFNs exhibited strong fluorescence under both wet and dry conditions, and no aggregation-caused quenching was observed in the solid state [52]. Furthermore, the original CFNs (40 mg/mL) exhibited a yellow color under natural light, while a strong green fluorescence emerged under UV irradiation in both acidic and neutral aqueous environments (Fig. S9). Additionally, a yellow–brown color was shown under natural light, while a weak pink fluorescence was observed under UV irradiation in a basic aqueous environment, thereby confirming the smart fluorescence of the CFNs. After 100 dilutions (0.4 mg/mL), the CFNs exhibited a light-yellow color under natural light (top panel, Fig. 3e) and a strong green fluorescence under UV irradiation (middle panel, Fig. 3e) in both acidic and neutral environments. The aforementioned observations were confirmed by the photographs with short exposure times (bottom panel, Fig. 3e). Comparatively, at pH = 11 and 13, the CFNs exhibited a yellowish-white color under natural light, pink fluorescence under UV irradiation, and blue fluorescence with a short exposure time, thereby demonstrating smart pH-sensitive color and fluorescence variations. Notably, CFNs showed a natural yellow color and strong cyan fluorescence at pH 9, whereas pristine curcumin exhibited a natural red color at this pH (Fig. S10). In addition, the results presented in Fig. S11 suggested that although curcumin was only partially dissolved in the basic solution (pH 13), it could still be successfully loaded into and precipitated with the nanoparticles. Therefore, the fluorescence of curcumin was effectively protected by the nanoparticles for various potential applications.

Following the methodologies described in previous studies [53,54,55], the as-fabricated strongly fluorescent CFNs were used to prepare fluorescent hydrogels/polymer coatings, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . The fluorescent hydrogels were obtained using various concentrations of CFNs (0, 10, 25, and 50 μL of CFNs in 1 mL of hydrogel precursor), and their photographs and fluorescent spectra were displayed in Fig. 4a and b. The as-prepared hydrogels exhibited good transparency under natural light, along with blue fluorescence under UV light for low concentrations of CFNs. Moreover, the turbidity of the gel increased with the concentration of CFNs. When higher concentration of CFNs was used (50 μL; Fig. 4a), green fluorescence was obtained in the hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of gels gradually increased with the increase of CFNs concentration, thereby confirming the smart and strong fluorescence of the CFNs in a high-polarity water-based environment. With the strong fluorescence, the hydrogels could be easily identified in water (Fig. S12) and suitable for underwater robotic applications. Interestingly, after the hydrogel dried, the strong fluorescence was retained in the hard “plastics” (Fig. S13). By adding a low polarity solvent (ethanol, polarity index: ∼5.4) [56], the polarity of the mixture solution was decreased, and the corresponding fluorescent intensity of the hydrogels was increased significantly by 30 % (Fig. S14). The good linear relationship between the illuminance change and the ethanol/water ratio indicated that the hydrogels could be used to evaluate the ethanol content in the ethanol/water mixture (Fig. S14b). The erasability of the fluorescence was demonstrated in Fig. 4c–f, where the green fluorescence quickly disappeared when the film was in contact with the ammonia solution (NH3·H2O) but reappeared with lower fluorescent intensity when the ammonia solution evaporated upon heating (Fig. 4c and 4e). The fluorescent color changed from green to blue when the film was subjected to a high temperature (100 °C) for 4 h with a significant fluorescent intensity decreasing (Fig. 4d and e). Three cycles of ammonia adding-heating releasing tests showed that although the fluorescence could reappear, the fluorescence intensity was weakened compared to the original sample, which could be due to the ammonia-assistant heat degradation of some curcumin (Fig. 4f), which could undergoes a fast degradation in neutral or alkaline medium [29]. But it was noteworthy that good smart fluorescence was still maintained even after three ammonia adding-heating releasing cycles. In addition, the aqueous CFNs precursor solution was readily incorporated into the transparent polymer coating, as shown in Fig. 4g. The resulting coating showed a transmittance greater than 90 % across the entire visible wavelength range of 400–800 nm. Thus, a covered logo (printed on paper) could be easily observed through a glass slide coated with the CFNs/PU film (inset). Furthermore, when the underlying features were completely obscured by an opaque, light-yellow film, the logo was clearly visible under UV light by using 20 μL of CFNs because of its distinct fluorescent color and intensity (Fig. 4h). Notably, the precursor solution could also be used to create sophisticated patterns or calligraphy directly on a substrate surface, and the cured precursor solution contained the designed information for anti-counterfeiting application (Fig. 4i). As shown in Fig. 4j, a CFNs/PU precursor solution (prepared as described in Section 2.4) was used to write letters on a glass substrate. After curing, the letters were not clearly visible under natural light (top row of the images of Fig. 4j), but they exhibited strong green fluorescence under UV light (middle row) and blue fluorescence under UV light with two droplets of water. The unique hydrophilic anti-counterfeiting feature is important to achieve a high level of security [57,58,59].

Finally, the smart fluorescent property was applied for food quality evaluation. As shown in Fig. 5 , a strong green fluorescence was observed (top row, Fig. 5a) when the CFNs/PU coating was placed in a bacterial culture medium (LB agar). In the presence of 104, 106, and 108 CFUs (other rows), the fluorescence was weakened significantly. This might be attributed to the bacteria changing the environmental pH values, leading to the fluorescent change. Thus, to further confirm the pH value, the bacteria were also incubated on LB agar and the pH was observed using pH test strips. As shown in Fig. S15, the LB agar without bacteria was neutral, while that with E. coli was alkaline, and the pH value increased with the number of bacteria, confirming that the alkaline environment led to the weakened fluorescence of CFNs/PU. The bar chart in Fig. 5b showed the measured fluorescence using a luminance meter after 12 h, demonstrating the bacteria-sensitive fluorescence intensity of the fabricated film. Thus, based on the pH- and bacteria-sensitive smart fluorescence properties, the as-fabricated CFNs solution was incorporated into a PU film and used in food bags and tags. When food proteins started to degrade, alkaline gases and moisture were generated with fungi growth [14,60,61]. These volatiles and moisture could be absorbed by the coating, changing the coating polarity and the fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5c, the deterioration of the pork was revealed by the fluorescence change from green to blue. As a control, pristine curcumin uniformly sprinkled on the surface of fresh pork for 2 days did not exhibit obvious fluorescence for both fresh (pH ∼ 6) and deteriorated pork (pH = 7–8) under UV light (Fig. S16), indicating that pristine curcumin could not monitor the meat quality in the range of neutral or weak alkalescence. Furthermore, compared with the fluorescent materials reported in the literature, the CFNs prepared in this study exhibited multi-color change and strong fluorescence in both solid and solution states. In addition, the green and sustainable preparation method only used water as a solvent, avoiding toxic organic solvents, such as toluene (Fig. 5d, Table S1).

In addition, common ions (such as Na+, K+, Cl− and SO4 2−), and organic substances (such as sugar and Vitamin C) in food were also used as models and dissolved in water and then introduced into CFNs. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, compared to the control, no obvious fluorescence change was observed. However, some multivalent metal ions (such as Fe3+ and Cu2+) could weaken or disrupt the fluorescence of CFNs (Fig. 6c–f) that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with an increase in Fe3+ and Cu2+ concentration, due to these ions could form metal complexes with curcumin [66], as shown in Fig. 6g. However, considering the small number of such metal ions in food, the overall fluorescence interference of multivalent metal ions on CFNs is negligible.

In this study, a novel organelle-inspired fluorescent film was developed based on sustainable and naturally fluorescent curcumin. Water-borne CFNs could be easily prepared on a large scale at a low cost via seeded emulsion polymerization without using toxic organic solvents. By varying the centrifugation speeds (e.g., 2500, 5000, 7500, and 10000 rpm), the fluorescent color of the CFNs was tuned from grass green through cyan to light blue, indicating their smart fluorescent performance. Importantly, strong fluorescence was observed in a high-water-content environment and a dry polymer coating, thereby solving the problem that the fluorescence of curcumin was highly dependent on the solvent polarity. The CFNs were incorporated into hydrogels and polymer coatings, which could maintain good transparency under natural light and show strong fluorescence under UV. Smart anti-counterfeiting and food quality monitoring applications have been demonstrated by facile and direct mixing or brushing of the CFNs precursors onto various labels or surfaces. Furthermore, the use of CFNs in polyurethane bags for visual food quality monitoring was demonstrated. This study provides valuable insight into the design of sustainable fluorescent labels and their applications in smart fluorescent hydrogels and polymer coatings for information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and food quality monitoring.
